region_of_origin: Global; commonly served in Singapore cafés, hawker dessert stalls, and hotel restaurants
food_subgroup: Full-fat dairy ice cream; 100g equals 2 standard café scoops; frozen dairy dessert

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vanilla ice cream as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 café scoops weighing approximately 100g, as commonly served at Singapore cafés, hawker dessert stalls, and hotel restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Vanilla Ice Cream — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167574)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Full-fat vanilla ice cream values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central for full-fat vanilla ice cream (approximately 10% fat content). All per-100g values preserved from the verified input.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — full-fat vanilla ice cream
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~51 for full-fat vanilla ice cream sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI ≤55 as low; therefore, GI 51 is classified as low. Despite high sugar content (21g/100g), the high fat content significantly slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. GL calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 23.6) / 100 ≈ 12.0 per 100g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat content (6.9g/100g) and moderate energy density
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 80mg per 2-scoop serve; however, the saturated fat content of 6.9g per serve is the primary cardiovascular concern for hypertensive patients, and ice cream should be limited to occasional consumption rather than a daily dietary item.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content (21g/100g) moderated by low GI (51) due to fat-slowed absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: With GI ~51 and GL ~12 per 2-scoop serve, postprandial glucose rise is more gradual than from sorbet or sugary drinks; however, the 21g sugar per serve (equivalent to approximately 5 teaspoons) requires careful portion control and should be counted within the total carbohydrate budget for patients with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (6.9g/100g) and trans fat (0.2g/100g) contributing to atherogenic lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Full-fat vanilla ice cream provides 6.9g saturated fat and 0.2g trans fat per 100g serve, which contributes meaningfully to the daily saturated fat limit of 20g for cardiovascular disease patients; consumption should be limited to occasional treats and replaced with lower-fat alternatives where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and high sugar both contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular consumption of full-fat ice cream is inconsistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary approach for rheumatoid arthritis; occasional consumption is acceptable, but patients should prioritise lower-sugar, lower-saturated-fat alternatives as part of an overall anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Full-fat vanilla ice cream served as 2 standard café scoops (100g) is a frozen dairy dessert made from heavy cream, whole milk, sugar, vanilla extract, and eggs. It provides 201 kcal, 11g total fat (6.9g saturated), 23.6g carbohydrate, 21g sugar, and 3.5g protein per 100g. The glycemic index is approximately 51 (low), which is lower than expected for a high-sugar food because the fat content significantly slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption — making full-fat ice cream less acutely glycaemia-raising than low-fat sorbet or sugary beverages. Calcium content is 128mg per serve, providing a minor nutritional benefit. Lactose content of approximately 4g per 100g means lactose-intolerant individuals may experience gastrointestinal symptoms from a 2-scoop serve; lactose-free ice cream or sorbet are suitable alternatives. In Singapore, vanilla ice cream is commonly served at cafés, hawker dessert stalls, and hotel restaurants, with popular brands including Walls, Haagen-Dazs, Ben and Jerry's, and local hawker ice cream. The food is classified as vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate GL of 12 per serve requires careful carbohydrate budgeting. For cardiovascular patients, the saturated fat content is the primary concern and warrants limiting consumption to occasional treats.
common_user_queries: can lactose intolerant eat ice cream
how many calories in 2 scoops ice cream

notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for vanilla ice cream used as the primary source; these values are not available from the approved source as of the access date. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry used. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — vanilla ice cream contains no dietary fibre by composition. GI category corrected to 'low' based on University of Sydney GI Database classification (≤55 is low). Halal status assumed based on standard full-fat dairy ice cream formulation with no pork-derived gelatin; individual brand verification recommended.
